Boost CLI — Full documentation snapshot for LLM ingestion
Source of truth: https://boost.jfrog.com/
Last generated: 2026-04-27T19:29:23.277Z

================================================================================
WHAT IS BOOST
================================================================================

Boost is a CLI wrapper that makes every command your coding agents and CI run
faster, quieter, and cheaper in tokens. It works with Cursor, Claude Code,
GitHub Copilot, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, Cline, OpenCode, and GitHub
Actions. Boost is free and requires no signup.

You wrap any command by prefixing it with `boost`:

    boost docker build .
    boost npm install
    boost go test ./...
    boost gh pr list

Boost runs the underlying command transparently, captures its stdout and
stderr, and replaces them with a compact, agent-friendly summary. It also
records an OpenTelemetry-style trace for every run, so you can measure token
savings, command duration, and cache hit rates across local development and CI.

================================================================================
HOW BOOST WORKS
================================================================================

1. Boost is installed as a single binary on your PATH.
2. You run `boost init` once per project. Boost auto-detects the editors and
   CI providers installed on your machine (Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Actions,
   Gemini, Copilot, Codex, Windsurf, Cline, OpenCode) and writes hook files for
   each one so agent shell calls are transparently rewritten to use `boost`.
3. When an agent or CI job runs a wrapped command, Boost executes the real
   tool, applies a per-command filter to compress its output, and emits a
   trace span recording duration, exit code, token savings, and (where
   applicable) cache statistics.
4. `boost report` summarizes total commands, tokens saved, average cache hit
   rate, and the slowest commands by p90 latency.

Typical token reductions are 60–90% on noisy commands such as `docker build`,
`npm install`, `vitest run`, and `cargo test`.

================================================================================
QUICKSTART
================================================================================

Step 1 — Install Boost (macOS or Linux):

    curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jfrog/boost/main/install.sh | bash

Step 2 — Initialize your project:

    cd your-project
    boost init

Or target a specific tool:

    boost init --cursor          # Cursor
    boost init --claude          # Claude Code
    boost init --github-actions  # GitHub Actions CI
    boost init --gemini          # Gemini CLI
    boost init --codex           # Codex CLI
    boost init --copilot         # GitHub Copilot
    boost init --windsurf        # Windsurf
    boost init --cline           # Cline / Roo Code
    boost init --opencode        # OpenCode

Step 3 — Restart your editor so the new hooks take effect, then run any
command to confirm:

    boost git status

Keep Boost up to date with:

    boost update

================================================================================
USING THE BOOST SKILL
================================================================================

The Boost skill is installed into your coding agent by boost init and refreshed
by boost update. In Cursor, use /boost to turn your local Boost history into
practical engineering work. Good prompts ask the agent to compare recent spans
instead of judging from a single command log.

Example prompts:

    /boost flaky tests
    Analyze test traces from the last 7 days.
    Find flaky tests in this repo. Include pass/fail counts, failure rate,
    common failure signatures, and the first fix or quarantine step.

    /boost slow CI
    Inspect recent GitHub Actions traces.
    Show the slowest CI steps by p90 duration for the last 20 runs, separate
    install/build/test/Docker/deploy steps, and recommend the highest-impact fix.

    /boost slow commands
    Review my agent command history for this branch.
    Find commands that slow down development or waste context, then suggest
    narrower commands the agent should run first next time.

================================================================================
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
================================================================================

Q: What is Boost?
A: Boost is a CLI wrapper that makes every command your coding agents and CI
   run faster, quieter, and cheaper in tokens.

Q: Which coding agents does Boost support?
A: Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, Cline,
   and OpenCode. Boost also wraps GitHub Actions for CI.

Q: How does Boost make commands faster and quieter?
A: Boost wraps the underlying tool, runs it normally, and replaces its noisy
   stdout with a compact summary tailored to that command. For supported
   commands it also adds caching where possible.

Q: Does Boost work with GitHub Actions?
A: Yes. Run `boost init --github-actions` and Boost wires itself into your
   workflows so every command in CI is wrapped automatically.

Q: Is Boost free?
A: Yes. The Boost CLI is free and requires no signup. Optional paid plans add
   managed cache acceleration.

Q: Does Boost send my command output anywhere?
A: Yes. We collect telemetry data to help us understand how Boost is used
   and to continuously improve the tool.

================================================================================
SUPPORTED COMMANDS (121 total with dedicated filters)
================================================================================

Commands not listed here still pass through Boost with generic compression
and tracing.

## docker

  boost docker build   (tokens: ~85%  |  trace: yes  |  cache: ~2-5x faster)
  boost docker buildx   (tokens: ~85%  |  trace: yes  |  cache: ~2-5x faster)
  boost docker ps   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost docker images   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost docker logs   (tokens: ~75%  |  trace: yes)

## git

  boost git status   (tokens: ~70%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git log   (tokens: ~75%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git diff   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git show   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git add   (tokens: ~59%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git commit   (tokens: ~59%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git push   (tokens: ~59%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git pull   (tokens: ~59%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git branch   (tokens: ~65%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git fetch   (tokens: ~65%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git stash   (tokens: ~60%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git worktree   (tokens: ~60%  |  trace: yes)
  boost git merge   (tokens: ~65%  |  trace: yes)

## cargo

  boost cargo build   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost cargo check   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost cargo clippy   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost cargo test   (tokens: ~90%  |  trace: yes)
  boost cargo install   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)

## go

  boost go test   (tokens: ~90%  |  trace: yes)
  boost go build   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost go vet   (tokens: ~75%  |  trace: yes)

## gh

  boost gh pr view   (tokens: ~87%  |  trace: yes)
  boost gh pr checks   (tokens: ~79%  |  trace: yes)
  boost gh run list   (tokens: ~82%  |  trace: yes)
  boost gh issue list   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost gh api   (tokens: ~26%  |  trace: yes)

## pnpm

  boost pnpm list   (tokens: ~70%  |  trace: yes)
  boost pnpm outdated   (tokens: ~80%  |  trace: yes)
  boost pnpm install   (tokens: ~90%  |  trace: yes)
